system_description.overview: Willow Care Inc. (known as Willow Voice) is a YC-backed AI-powered voice-to-text software designed to feel hyper-personalized and effortless across all apps. It learns each user's unique style, tone, and corrections over time. The Mac product integrates seamlessly into workflows including Messages, Slack, Gmail, and Docs.

products.Willow Voice Mac Application: Secure, high-speed voice-to-text transcription directly on macOS; integrates with productivity and communication apps; local client features capture audio, send dictation requests, and deliver transcriptions.
products.Backend Infrastructure & APIs: Cloud-hosted services that process audio and deliver transcription results to the Mac client; provides secure communication, storage, and data management; includes monitoring, logging, and fault-tolerant design.

infrastructure.cloud_provider: Supabase (carve-out — Supabase controls excluded from scope)
infrastructure.physical_presence: None — no physical offices; all data processed on cloud services
infrastructure.availability_zones: Multi-AZ (production servers at Supabase maintain failover capabilities; high availability data centers with multiple availability zones)
infrastructure.cdn: Cloudflare (used for JWT verification and traffic routing per network diagram)

network_architecture.network_segmentation: System hosted in Supabase VPC environment; segmented VPCs and ACLs; network segmentation implemented to separate systems and data based on sensitivity and criticality
network_architecture.firewalls: System firewalls configured on application gateway and production network to limit unnecessary ports, protocols, and services; firewall rules reviewed annually by IT management
network_architecture.ids_ips: IDS and IPS employed via Supabase to identify and protect against threats; intrusion detection system used to monitor and detect unauthorized access
network_architecture.vpn: Remote access done through a bastion host with a VPN
network_architecture.tls: TLS used for all web-based communication; certificates from established third-party certificate authority
network_architecture.content_filtering: All systems restricted to internet via content filtering system routed through proxy server
network_architecture.topology: Client Application sends Active JWT + Audio File -> Cloudflare (JWT Verification) -> Audio File -> Baseten (audio processing) -> Transcription -> Groq (LLM formatting) -> Formatted Transcription -> Client Application -> Updated Word Count -> Supabase (database/storage)

application_architecture.pattern: Hybrid (Mac native client + cloud backend pipeline)
application_architecture.environments: Separate development/test and production environments; changes tested before deployment to Stage or Production

data_storage.backup_frequency: Daily (backups taken daily; multi-availability zones configured)

authentication_access_control.identity_provider: Supabase (password policy controlled through Supabase Password policy)
authentication_access_control.mfa: MFA enforced for all user accounts with administrative access to production platform; MFA enforced for all remote access; source code tool restricted to authorized users using MFA
authentication_access_control.access_revocation_sla: Within one business day upon termination
authentication_access_control.password_policy: Periodic forced changes, password expiry and complexity requirements enforced via Supabase Password policy; account lockout after limited unsuccessful logon attempts

encryption.at_rest: Cloud provider key management service used (Supabase KMS)
encryption.key_management: Cloud provider key management service; access to encryption keys restricted to authorized personnel

ci_cd_devops.source_control: Not named (source code management tool referenced; MFA-protected)
ci_cd_devops.branch_protection: Code repository branch rules configured to require additional approval for every merge request to move change to production; developers cannot push directly to production
ci_cd_devops.iac: Baseline configurations retained within infrastructure as code; configuration management tool used for implementation and rollback
ci_cd_devops.patch_management: Automatic patch management set up by CTO; monthly patch management reviews; workstations configured for automatic updates; daily patch compliance scanning

monitoring_logging.apm_tool: Not named (monitoring tool referenced that generates alerts at predefined thresholds)
monitoring_logging.log_aggregation: Logging enabled for administrative activities, logon attempts, data deletions, changes to functions, security configurations, permissions, and roles; log protection in place
monitoring_logging.alerting: Automated alerts configured to notify IT management of high and critical risk events; tracked until resolved within change-management/ticketing application

third_party_services.purpose: Primary cloud infrastructure — database, storage, networking, VPC, physical data center
JWT verification, CDN/traffic layer between client and backend
ML model inference for audio-to-text transcription processing

security_tools.antivirus: Anti-virus and Malware protection software installed on all production servers and Mac workstations/laptops; auto-scan on continuous basis; definitions updated weekly on servers, daily via vendor website
security_tools.vulnerability_scanning: Monthly automated vulnerability scans on infrastructure and applications; scan frequency adjusted as needed
security_tools.penetration_testing: Annually by independent third-party; high/critical findings remediated in timely manner

untestable: CC2.2.3 — no significant personnel changes made during engagement period
CC3.2.1 — cybersecurity insurance could not be tested because no cyber incidents reported during period
CC7.3.1 — incident response could not be tested because no security incidents reported during period
CC6.5.2 — customer data deletion could not be tested because no customer terminations during period
excluded_criteria: CC6.4 — physical access to data centers; Supabase is responsible for physical security
A1.2 — availability environmental protections; Willow Care does not have office/physical servers; managed by building management/Supabase
notable_controls: Quarterly user access reviews for all accounts including admin-privileged accounts
Daily backup with multi-AZ configuration confirmed by auditor
Network diagram with Cloudflare JWT verification layer explicitly documented
BCDR tested annually with specific loss scenarios

infrastructure.rationale: Supabase-hosted with multi-AZ failover and Cloudflare CDN; IaC baseline confirmed. Single provider dependency (Supabase) with carve-out limiting audit coverage. No multi-region. Regions not specified.

application_architecture.rationale: Clear pipeline architecture documented with distinct services (Cloudflare, Baseten, Groq, Supabase). Branch protection and separate environments confirmed. Languages/frameworks not disclosed. No container orchestration mentioned.

data_layer.rationale: Daily backups confirmed with multi-AZ. Supabase KMS for encryption at rest. Data classification and retention policies exist. No caching layer or data warehouse disclosed. Limited specifics on database technology.

security_posture.rationale: Annual third-party pen testing, monthly vuln scans, IDS/IPS, MFA enforced, anti-malware on all endpoints, cybersecurity insurance in place. Firewall rules reviewed annually. No WAF explicitly called out. SIEM not mentioned.

devops_maturity.rationale: IaC confirmed, branch protection with required approvals, separate environments, emergency change process. CI/CD tool unnamed. Container orchestration not mentioned. Automated patch management in place.

bcdr_readiness.rationale: BCDR policy documented and annually reviewed and tested. Daily backups with multi-AZ. Continuity via spinning up multiple servers across all AZs in Supabase. No multi-region failover. RTO/RPO not disclosed.

vendor_diversity.rationale: 4 named vendors (Supabase, Cloudflare, Baseten, Groq). Critical single-point dependency on Supabase for infrastructure. Interesting use of specialized AI inference vendors (Baseten + Groq) showing modern ML pipeline thinking.

overall.rationale: Clean SOC 2 Type 2 with zero exceptions and a well-documented network architecture for a small YC company. Strong security process hygiene. Main weaknesses: heavy Supabase dependency with carve-out, no named CI/CD tooling, no SIEM, RTO/RPO undisclosed.

red_flags: Heavy single-vendor dependency on Supabase for all infrastructure (compute, DB, storage, networking); controls at Supabase are carved out and not audited
No CI/CD tool named — cannot assess pipeline security or automation maturity
No SIEM mentioned — log aggregation appears manual/alerting-only
yellow_flags: Multi-region failover not implemented — single Supabase region (unspecified)
No WAF explicitly mentioned (Cloudflare is present but only referenced for JWT verification, not WAF function)
Programming languages and frameworks not disclosed — tech stack opaque
Trust criteria limited to Security only — Availability and Confidentiality not in scope
green_flags: Clean audit with zero exceptions across all 57 tested controls
Annual third-party penetration testing confirmed by auditor
MFA enforced for all admin access and remote access
Daily backups with multi-AZ confirmed and evidence-tested by auditor
Quarterly user access reviews including privileged accounts
Clear network diagram published showing full AI pipeline (Cloudflare -> Baseten -> Groq -> Supabase)
BCDR annually tested with loss scenario coverage
Server hardening with IaC baseline configuration management
key_observations: Architecture diagram reveals a notable 4-hop AI pipeline: Client -> Cloudflare (JWT auth) -> Baseten (ASR/audio inference) -> Groq (LLM post-processing) -> Supabase (storage/DB). This is an unusually transparent disclosure of the full ML inference stack.
Groq usage for transcription post-processing is notable — Groq provides low-latency LLM inference, suggesting performance-sensitive real-time pipeline design.
Despite being a small YC company, the governance structure includes a formal Risk and Governance Executive Committee (RGEC) meeting semiannually — unusually mature for a seed-stage company.
Supabase itself has a SOC 2 report which Willow Care reviews annually — this is good vendor management practice even with carve-out treatment.
The report covers Security only (Processing Integrity and Privacy determined not relevant) — appropriate for a dictation product but limits assurance depth for healthcare/sensitive-data buyers.
No SSO mentioned — customer authentication method not described in detail; relies on Supabase's password policy infrastructure.
